title: The Battle That Ended an Empire
subtitle: Yorktown, 1781
tagline: For six years, the most powerful military on Earth had been grinding down a rebellion. Then France arrived. Then Washington marched. Then Cornwallis ran out of options.
villain_mandate: Britain must feel genuinely dominant throughout. Six years of victories. The most professional army in the world. The largest navy. Washington's losses must feel real — Valley Forge, the near-collapse of the Continental Army. The Yorktown trap must feel against-impossible-odds.

scenes.title: Six Years of Losing
scenes.narration: 1781. The American Revolution had been going for six years. The Continental Army had been destroyed twice, rebuilt twice, and was surviving mostly on French money and Washington's refusal to quit. Britain controlled New York, the largest port in North America. They controlled the sea. Their professional army had beaten Washington in every major engagement except Trenton and Princeton. Then the French sent a fleet. And Washington saw his chance.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The American Revolution's six years of struggle — Valley Forge shown: starving soldiers, frozen ground, Washington moving among them. British forces in New York, professional, well-equipped. The imbalance of forces visible. Then: French ships on the horizon. Washington receiving intelligence, looking at a map of Virginia. Something has changed. 16:9.

scenes.title: Cornwallis in Virginia
scenes.narration: General Lord Cornwallis had marched his army of eight thousand soldiers north through the Carolinas, winning battles and losing the war — the countryside wouldn't pacify, the guerrillas wouldn't stop, the territory wouldn't hold. In the summer of 1781, on orders from General Clinton in New York, he marched his army to Yorktown, Virginia — a small tobacco port on the York River — and began building fortifications. Clinton had promised the navy would supply him. Clinton had the only British fleet in America.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Cornwallis — experienced, proud, frustrated by a war that won't end. His army marching north through the Carolinas, winning battles that don't matter. Arriving at Yorktown: a small tobacco port, the York River behind it. Cornwallis building fortifications, expecting naval resupply and possible evacuation. He is not worried. He should be. 16:9.

scenes.title: Washington's Deception
scenes.narration: Washington was in New York, opposite Clinton's main British force. He began extensive preparations for an assault on New York — fortifications, supply movements, troop exercises visible to British spies. Clinton was convinced Washington was going to attack New York. He kept his forces concentrated. He did not send reinforcements south. In secret, Washington marched his entire army south toward Virginia. By the time Clinton realized what was happening, Washington had a week's head start.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Washington's deception — fake fortifications going up near New York, supply wagons moving toward the city, visible to British observers. Clinton watching and consolidating in New York. Then: the secret night march south — Washington's entire army moving quietly, away from New York, toward Virginia. The week's head start building. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Battle of the Chesapeake
scenes.narration: September 5, 1781. The British fleet arrived to resupply Cornwallis. The French fleet — nineteen ships of the line — was already there. For two hours, the French and British fleets fought the Battle of the Chesapeake. No ships sank. No dramatic victory. The British fleet withdrew to New York for repairs. But the result was absolute: the British navy could not reach Yorktown. Cornwallis was cut off by sea. Washington was coming by land. The trap was closed.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The Battle of the Chesapeake — nineteen French ships of the line against the British fleet. Cannon fire, ships maneuvering, the two-hour engagement. The British fleet withdrawing — damaged but not destroyed. The significance: not the ships lost but the sea lane closed. The map of Yorktown now showing water on three sides, French ships blocking resupply, Washington coming from the north. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Siege Begins
scenes.narration: October 6, 1781. Washington's combined Franco-American force of seventeen thousand soldiers — twice Cornwallis's eight thousand — began the siege of Yorktown. Three weeks earlier, Cornwallis had been confident. Now he was trapped. The siege approached methodically: parallel trenches dug closer each day, artillery positioned, the range tightening. On the night of October 14, Alexander Hamilton led an assault on two key British redoubts. Both fell in ten minutes.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The siege of Yorktown — the methodical approach, parallel trenches advancing nightly, artillery batteries being positioned. The Franco-American force's organization: French artillery (exceptional) and American infantry working together. Hamilton leading the night assault on Redoubt 10 — ten minutes of brutal fighting, the redoubt taken. The noose tightening. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Last Options
scenes.narration: Cornwallis attempted to break out across the York River at night — his soldiers in boats, crossing to Gloucester Point where he could break through the smaller French force and march north. A storm scattered the boats. His last option: gone. He sent a message to his officers: 'I propose to give this place up to Foe.' On October 17, 1781 — four years to the day after the British surrender at Saratoga — he asked for terms.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Cornwallis's desperate breakout attempt — boats loading at night, soldiers crossing the river. Then: the storm, boats scattered, men turning back. Cornwallis receiving his officers, the letter being written. October 17: the white flag going up over Yorktown. Four years to the day after Saratoga. 16:9.

scenes.title: The World Turned Upside Down
scenes.narration: October 19, 1781. The British army marched out of Yorktown to surrender. Cornwallis claimed illness and sent a subordinate. The British band played a song called 'The World Turned Upside Down.' Washington refused to accept the surrender from a subordinate — he made them surrender to his own second-in-command, General Lincoln, who had been forced to surrender at Charleston the year before. It was exact. It was deliberate.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The British surrender ceremony — the British army marching out in full uniform. Cornwallis absent, his subordinate carrying the sword. Washington on horseback, refusing to receive from a subordinate, directing to Lincoln. The British band playing 'The World Turned Upside Down.' The defeated army marching between lines of French and American soldiers. 16:9.

scenes.title: The War That Continued
scenes.narration: Yorktown did not end the Revolutionary War legally — that took two more years of diplomacy, the Treaty of Paris in 1783. The British still held New York. They still had a larger navy. They could have continued. But Yorktown broke Britain's political will. Prime Minister Lord North received the news and said: 'Oh God, it is all over.' Parliament refused to fund further war. Britain negotiated. America won.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. London receiving the news of Yorktown — Lord North in Parliament, the report arriving. His face. The Parliamentary vote refusing further funds. Diplomats in Paris: the treaty negotiations, the map of America being drawn. Britain's navy still intact, its army unbroken, its political will gone. The war ending not with defeat but with refusal to continue. 16:9.

scenes.title: Six Years
scenes.narration: Washington never had a fully equipped, fully paid, fully supplied army. He never outnumbered the British in a major engagement except Yorktown. He lost more battles than he won. His strategy — keep the army alive, avoid decisive defeat, wait for an opportunity — was not glorious. But it worked. Yorktown was not a brilliant military victory. It was the payoff of six years of refusal to lose.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Washington across six years — Valley Forge, the retreats, the small victories at Trenton and Princeton, the years of holding on. Then: Yorktown, the opportunity arrived, Washington moving his entire army in secret to meet it. The six years of survival made visible as preparation for one perfect moment. 16:9.

scenes.title: The World Turned Upside Down
scenes.narration: Britain had the most powerful military on Earth. They won more battles than they lost. They never lost the naval superiority that was supposed to guarantee their supply lines — until one afternoon in the Chesapeake, when a French fleet made them withdraw. Everything else followed from that. The most powerful empire in the world lost a colony because a fleet showed up on the right day. Subscribe to Gods & Glory for the battles that changed everything.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Final card — Yorktown today: the battlefield, the earthworks still visible, the monument. The York River behind it, where the breakout boats turned back. The place where an empire decided it was finished with a war it was still capable of fighting. 16:9.

viral_hook: Britain had the most powerful military on Earth. They were winning. Then France sent a fleet, Washington marched south in secret, and Cornwallis ran out of options.
highlight_note: Scene 7 — the surrender ceremony. Washington's exact deliberate move with Lincoln. The band plays 'The World Turned Upside Down.'
youtube_title: The Battle That Ended the British Empire in America | Yorktown 1781 | Gods & Glory
lesson: Yorktown wasn't won in a day. It was won over six years of refusing to lose, waiting for the moment when every variable aligned.
